speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Moishe Yosef Weisel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got the picture from my brother. And he was in the Hungarian labor force, and he had this picture. And I don't know when it was taken and where.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have other pictures of your family at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the only one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only one

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of me, Matilda Klein. It was taken in 1943, when I was 16, but I don't remember who took it and for what purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Svalava, in my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1945, in Prague, Czechoslovakia. On the right is my brother, Issac Weisel, and on the left is our Uncle Alex Weisel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the left, is he in uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Czech Army. He was a officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why the picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: For fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, I kept them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Karlovy Vary, Czechoslovakia. It was a May parade in the national costume.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The May parade is May 1.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are all those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right on the right side,

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right side. Which one from the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one? The first one. No, the first. This one.

speaker: interviewer
text: That one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And you are wearing the white skirt.

speaker: interviewee
text: National costume. No this was a apron.

speaker: interviewer
text: An apron with a black top?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a vest

speaker: interviewer
text: A vest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And something on the head, some flowers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: May parade

speaker: interviewer
text: A May parade for-- for Czechoslovakia, or for the communist groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm the person in the front. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Dvory, Karlovy Vary. It's Czechoslovakia, on November, '45 in front of the house. To the right is my brother Eliezer and to the left is a cousin, Joseph [? Yakobovich ?] was his name, but now he's Jacobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any special reason for taking this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This picture was taken in front of the house where we lived, in Dvory, Karlovy Vary, Czechoslovakia, in the month of November, 1945. To the right is my brother, Eliezer, and to the left is Joseph [? Yakobovich ?]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the front, the girl is Naama Saltzmann. She's my granddaughter. She lives in Jerusalem. And the picture is taken in the bar mitzvah for Yair. Next to her is Yair, her brother, and Dan, her brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in back of Dan?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the back of Dan is his father, Sydney Saltzmann, and his wife, Eva. This is my daughter. And the son Uzi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Year-- two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In front is sitting my husband, Ernest Klein. Next to him it's me. And then it's Alan, and his father, Tommy, and Brian is 13, and my daughter, Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this is taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1990, in the bar mitzvah of Brian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in the bar mitzvah of Brian. And here is my daughter-in-law, Brenda, and the baby, Kory. And in the green dress is me, and my husband, Ernest, and Josh, my only son in the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was at Brian's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the bar mitzvah in-- in '90, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In where? In--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Start with whomever you want. You want to start with this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Then start with there. And then go across like this. And in back of them, the second row standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The children, I don't know them. Should I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just say you don't know them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother's grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell them it's your brother's children-- grandchildren. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left is my brother, Issac Weisel, my oldest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he sitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is sitting, yes. Next to him is Noah Weisel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is Noah Weisel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another brother. And then it's Eliezer with the baby, his grandchildren. And behind him is his daughter-in-law, [? Razzi ?]. And then it's my husband, me, Hannah, my sister-in-law, and Rachel, my sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were these taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years, ago,

speaker: interviewer
text: About two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in New York someplace. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my son Josh, and his lovely wife Brenda, and I love them very much. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is Kory Klein and Adriel Klein, and they are the children of Josh and Brenda. And they live in Boston. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how old are they there?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Kory Klein, age five, and Adriel Klein, age three, and they are the children of Josh and Brenda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mrs. Klein, you now have your whole-- part of your family with you. We'd like it very much if you could introduce them. And then we can ask them a few questions. And tell us a little bit more about your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my brother, Eliezer Weisel, and this is my husband, Ernest. Don't cry. And this is Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sarah is who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah is the daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is Sarah's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Major.

speaker: interviewer
text: Major?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and this is my sister-in-law, Rachel. It's his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for joining us. I'd like to start with you, Sarah, if possible. How much of your parents' life, or your mother's life, actually, is impacted in your growing up, and in your-- your own life and the way you're raising your children, et cetera?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure it did impact my life. But interestingly enough, all my friends are in the same situation that I am. They're all children of survivors. So we have a lot in common, in a sense, that we think of our parents in-- in the same way. I don't know how I ended up picking up friends that way, but it's-- it's a funny thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As far as my children are concerned, the differences that I recognize, are that when I hear Holocaust stories, it upsets me a great deal. And I think my children are little bit more removed from it. It doesn't upset them as much when they studied in school, when they read about it in great detail, they said, it's sad. But for me, it gives me nightmares.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't think about it as much anymore as I did when I was younger, when I was around 16, 17. As my mother would tell me stories, and the stories were not that horrible. She would definitely censor them. But for me, it was horrible to think that my own mother and my father and everybody else went through these horrible, terrible, things. When did you know about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been a teenager, 14, 15, 16. And as time went on, my mother would tell me more and more stories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How about you, Mr. Klein? How was your life in America, or in Israel, affected by your experiences, yours and your wife's experiences in the Holocaust? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I expect my wife [INAUDIBLE] her is--

speaker: interviewee
text: He can't talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You, Mr. Weisel, were separated from your sister during the war. Would you care to add anything to-- to her story, or to your experiences together as brother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I didn't have a story, but I want-- I want to mention the last chapter of her story when she came back from the concentration camp. I was-- I was already at home, what we called home. And I find out that she is alive. And she was already in the next-- it was there, a nearby-- another city, where she-- she got-- she got there. And I find out that she is there. She is coming home, afternoon. And there wasn't any communication at this time, to-- to one city to the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came home with a truck. And I was waiting. And I saw her for the first time in about one and a half year. I remember, I wanted something to ask, and I couldn't ask. I just took her by the hand, and went with her to a certain place where we was living. I was holding her hand the whole-- the whole way. And then I turned to her and asked one question. Where did the mother go? The whole time we didn't believe that's really what happened. We heard, but we didn't believe. Well, we was hoping, maybe something-- it's not true what-- what happened But I hear from her. And I asked her, what happened? She said, she went to the left side. That-- that was the first time that I was capable to cry. Would you like to add anything to end your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't I tell you the same thing?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes you did tell me the same thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you two never talked over what happened to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, ever. Never, ever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never asked her anymore about it since then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just said, didn't you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She told it. I didn't hear about that war. And I didn't want to know any war.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wanted to hear it. Yeah, I told the same story.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with your other brother you also never discussed details of what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Practically-- practically, I don't-- I don't read anything about the Holocaust. I don't want to see any films about the Holocaust. Even if I am going-- I went to Yad Vashem. Yesterday I was at the Holocaust in Washington. I can just walk over, at what fast that it's possible, and out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to add anything, Mrs. Klein, before we close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I wanted to talk about-- they ask me about birth certificate. I don't have any. Old pictures, I don't have any. Some mementos from the old home, we don't have any. And how you say, [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Grave.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grave, we don't have any. Like we didn't exist.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Eliezer has pictures. He was in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. But here, can we go to-- like now before Yom Kippur, everybody goes for [? Carveros. ?] We have no place to go. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for joining us, and thank you for sharing your story.
